mnemonics.title: Linear Transformation Check
mnemonics.content: For any transformation T, to verify it's linear, use TEST: T(u+v) equals T(u)+T(v), and T(cv) equals c×T(v). If both hold, it's linear as a rock!

mnemonics.title: Kernel-Remember
mnemonics.content: KERNEL: Keep In mind - it lives in the DOMAIN (input space). Like the kernel of corn is inside the cob!

mnemonics.title: Rank-Nullity
mnemonics.content: RANK + NULLITY = DIMENSION. Think: The Total (domain) = Working (range) + Not-Working (kernel). Everything either gets transformed meaningfully or collapses to zero.

acronyms.expansion: Vectors that get crushed to ZERO in the output space
acronyms.usage: Ker(T) = {v | T(v) = 0}, always a subspace of domain

acronyms.expansion: Real output vectors we can ACHIEVE
acronyms.usage: Range(T) = {w | w = T(v) for some v}, always a subspace of codomain

acronyms.expansion: We can REACH everything in codomain
acronyms.usage: For every w∈W, there exists v∈V such that T(v)=w, Range(T)=W

visualTips.visualization: Think of transformation as input vector × matrix = output vector. Columns of matrix tell where each basis vector lands. Draw arrows from basis vectors to their images.
Imagine all vectors in kernel collapsing to the origin like a black hole. The dimension of kernel tells how much 'crushing' happens.
If range fills entire codomain, it's onto. If range is a line (1D) in 2D codomain, it's not onto. Think of a flashlight beam - can it light up the whole wall?
If you drop a rubber sheet and map points, one-to-one means no two different points end up at the same spot - no overlapping or squishing.

analogies.analogy: Like a specialized machine in a factory: it takes raw materials (input vectors), processes them predictably (linear rules), and produces finished products (output vectors). Same input always gives same output.
The 'trash' or 'waste' that gets destroyed - inputs that become nothing but zero. In a movie projector, the broken pixels are like kernel - they contribute nothing to the final image.
Think of a 3D object projected onto a 2D screen. The 'rank' is the useful 2D information (2), the 'nullity' is the depth information lost (1). Total 3 = 2 + 1.

Two vector spaces are isomorphic if there's a perfect translation dictionary between them - like English and French have different words but same grammatical structure. They're essentially the same space in different clothing.
